title: प्रातःस्मरण स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री प्रातःस्मरण स्तोत्रम् ॥

प्रातः स्मरामि हृदि संस्फुरदात्मतत्त्वं
सच्चित्सुखं परमहंसगतिं तुरीयम्।
यत्स्वप्नजागरसुषुप्तमवैति नित्यं
तद्ब्रह्म निष्कलमहं न च भूतसङ्घः॥१॥

प्रातर्भजामि मनसा वचसामगम्यं
वाचो विभान्ति निखिला यदनुग्रहेण।
यन्नेतिनेतिवचनैर्निगमा अवोचन्
तद्देवदेवमजमच्युतमाहुरग्र्यम्॥२॥

प्रातर्नमामि तमसः परमर्कवर्णं
पूर्णं सनातनपदं पुरुषोत्तमाख्यम्।
यस्मिन्निदं जगदशेषमशेषमूर्तौ
रज्ज्वां भुजङ्गम इव प्रतिभासितं वै॥३॥

॥ फलश्रुति ॥
श्लोकत्रयमिदं पुण्यं लोकत्रयविभूषणम्।
प्रातःकाले पठेद्यस्तु स गच्छेत्परमं पदम्॥

॥ इति श्रीमच्छंकराचार्यविरचितं प्रातःस्मरणस्तोत्रं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Pratah Smarana Stotram ||

Praatah Smaraami Hridi Samsphuradaatmatattvam
SachChitsukham Paramahamsagatim Tureeyam.
Yatsvapnajaagara sushuptamavaiti Nityam
Tadbrahma Nishkalamaham Na Cha Bhootasanghah.||1||

Praatarbhajaami Manasaa Vachasaamagamyam
Vaacho Vibhaanti Nikhilaa Yadanugrahen​a.
Yanneti Netivachanair​nigamaa Avochan
Taddevadevamajamachy​utamaahuragry​am.||2||

Praatarnamamai Tamasah Paramarka​varnam
Poornam Sanaatanapadam Purushottamaakhyam.
Yasminnidam Jagadasheshamashesha​moortau
Rajjvaam Bhujangama Iva Pratibhaasitam Vai.||3||

|| Phalashruti ||
Shlokatrayamidam Punyam Lokatrayavibhooshanam.
Praatakaale Pathedyastu Sa Gachchhetparamam Padam.

|| Iti Shrimachhankaraacharyavirachitam Praatahsmaranaastotram Sampoornam ||
hindiMeaning:
(1) प्रातःकाल मैं हृदय में स्फुरित होने वाले आत्मतत्त्व का स्मरण करता हूँ — जो सत्-चित्-सुख रूप है, परमहंसों की गति (लक्ष्य) है, तुरीय (चतुर्थ अवस्था) है। जो स्वप्न, जाग्रत और सुषुप्ति को नित्य जानता है — वह निष्कल ब्रह्म मैं हूँ, भूत-संघ (शरीर) नहीं।

(2) प्रातःकाल मैं मन से उसे भजता हूँ जो वाणी से अगम्य है, जिसकी अनुग्रह से सम्पूर्ण वाणी विभासित होती है। जिसे वेदों ने 'नेति-नेति' (यह नहीं, यह नहीं) कहा — उस देवदेव, अज, अच्युत, अग्र्य (सर्वश्रेष्ठ) को भजता हूँ।

(3) प्रातःकाल मैं तमस् से परे, सूर्य-वर्ण, पूर्ण, सनातन-पद, पुरुषोत्तम नाम वाले को नमस्कार करता हूँ — जिसमें यह सम्पूर्ण जगत अशेषमूर्ति में रज्जु में सर्प के समान प्रतिभासित (भ्रमवश दिखाई देता) है।

यह तीन श्लोकों का पुण्य स्तोत्र तीनों लोकों का विभूषण है। जो प्रातःकाल पढ़ता है, वह परमपद प्राप्त करता है।
benefits: Shankaracharya's ideal morning meditation prayer - just three profound verses
First verse: Smarana (remembrance) of Atman as Sat-Chit-Sukha Turiya
Second verse: Bhajana (worship) of the Neti-Neti Brahman beyond speech
Third verse: Namana (salutation) to Purushottama, in whom the world is illusory
Invokes paramam padam (supreme abode) when recited at dawn
Perfect Advaita meditation encapsulating Shankaracharya's entire philosophy
description: The Pratah Smarana Stotram, known in Sanskrit as प्रातःस्मरण स्तोत्रम्, is a devotional hymn of praise in praise of Lord Vishnu. This revered text originates from Adi Shankaracharya. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to shankaracharya' ideal morning meditation prayer - just three profound verses as well as grant Paramam Padam (supreme abode) when recited at dawn. This sacred text is ideally recited on Daily for enhanced blessings. Comprising 3 verses, it can be completed in approximately 3 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Pratah Smarana Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Pratah Smarana Stotram by Shankaracharya in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Three-verse morning prayer for Atma-jnana and Brahman realization.
relatedSearches: Pratah Smaranam Adi Shankaracharya three verses
Pratah Smarana Stotram lyrics with English meaning
Best Sanskrit shloka to chant in the morning
Brahma Muhurta morning prayer routine Hindu
Adi Shankara stotras list with meaning

seoFaqs.question: What is Pratah Smarana Stotram and who composed it?
seoFaqs.answer: Pratah Smarana Stotram is a three-verse morning prayer composed by Adi Shankaracharya in the eighth century CE. The title literally means 'remembrance at dawn'. The three verses are uniquely structured so that the first dedicates the mind (manas), the second dedicates the speech (vak) and the third dedicates the body (kaya) of the practitioner to Brahman, the supreme Reality. By internalising these three offerings as the very first thoughts, words and actions of the day, the practitioner aligns the entire day's activity with the non-dual goal of Advaita Vedanta. The stotram is therefore both a prayer and a compressed lesson in Shankara's philosophy. (Sources: Kamakoti; Vedanta Students.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the philosophical content of the three verses?
seoFaqs.answer: The first verse contemplates Brahman as Saccidananda (existence-consciousness-bliss), the witness of the three states of waking, dream and deep-sleep, and identifies the practitioner's true Self with that partless Brahman. The second verse negates identification with body and mind, declaring the Self to be free of caste, gotra, family and the four ashramas. The third verse describes Brahman as the source and substratum of the world, which appears yet has no independent reality. Together the three condense the central Vedantic teaching of jeevatma-paramatma-aikya (unity of individual and universal Self) into a recitation of under three minutes. (Sources: Shlokam; Arsha Vidya Gurukulam.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is dawn (pratah) considered the most powerful time for this stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: The classical Indian time-cycle reserves the period from approximately ninety-six minutes to forty-eight minutes before sunrise as Brahma Muhurta, the hour of Brahman. Sattva guna is held to be naturally dominant in the atmosphere at this hour, the mind is least agitated by the previous day's impressions, and the breath rhythms are slow and even. Dawn is also presented in Vedic literature as the outer symbol of inner spiritual awakening, the moment when light dispels darkness. Recitation at dawn therefore plants the day's first seed-impression at the deepest layer of the mind, conditioning subsequent thought and action toward sattva. (Sources: Vedanta Students; Shlokam.)

seoFaqs.question: How does Pratah Smarana Stotram differ from other morning shlokas like 'Karagre Vasate Lakshmi'?
seoFaqs.answer: Karagre Vasate Lakshmi is a short two-line shloka that visualises Lakshmi at the fingertips, Saraswati at the palm-centre and Govinda at the wrist, and is recited the moment one looks at one's own palms upon waking. It is devotional and iconographic. Pratah Smarana Stotram, by contrast, is a longer Vedantic meditation that follows the bath and the lighting of the lamp, and is contemplative rather than iconographic. Many traditional households use both: Karagre upon opening the eyes, Bhumi-Vandana when stepping out of bed, and Pratah Smaranam after the morning ablutions before any other recitation begins. (Sources: greenmesg; Shlokam.)

seoFaqs.question: Are there parallel Pratah Smaranam stotrams for specific deities?
seoFaqs.answer: Yes. Beyond Shankara's Vedantic Pratah Smaranam, the tradition preserves Vishnu Pratah Smaranam, Shiva Pratah Smaranam, Devi Pratah Smaranam, Ganesha Pratah Smaranam, Surya Pratah Smaranam and Datta Pratah Smaranam, each addressed to the respective deity and recited at dawn by adherents of those traditions. The Shiva Pratah Smaranam, in particular, is widely chanted in Smarta and Veera-Shaiva households. The structure mirrors Shankara's original: dedication of mind, speech and body, but to a saguna (with-form) deity rather than to nirguna Brahman. Practitioners often recite the Vedantic version followed by their lineage's saguna version. (Sources: greenmesg Shiva; Shlokam Shiva Pratah.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the recommended posture and procedure for daily recitation?
seoFaqs.answer: Traditional procedure: rise during Brahma Muhurta, look at one's palms while reciting Karagre Vasate Lakshmi, touch the earth with Bhumi-Vandana, complete morning ablutions and bath, then sit facing east on a clean asana of wool, deer-skin or kusha grass. Spine erect, eyes lightly closed, hands in dhyana mudra resting in the lap, and breath even. Recite the three verses of Pratah Smaranam slowly, pausing between verses to internalise the meaning. Conclude with a moment of silent meditation before commencing other japa or daily duties. The full sequence takes ten to fifteen minutes and is regarded as the minimum sandhya for a householder. (Sources: Arsha Vidya Gurukulam; Vedanta Students.)
